---
title: "Examining the Influence of Socioeconomic Factors on Premature Death Rates Across Racial Groups"
description: "Used statistical modeling in R to analyze county-level socioeconomic predictors of premature mortality and health disparities. Completed during the Carnegie Mellon Summer Undergraduate Research Experience (SURE) program with UnitedHealth Group." 
date: July 2024
about:
  template: solana
  image: cover.jpeg
  links:
    - icon: laptop
      text: Slides
      href: https://www.stat.cmu.edu/cmsac/sure/2024/showcase/premature_deaths/slides.pdf
    - icon: file
      text: Poster
      href: https://www.stat.cmu.edu/cmsac/sure/2024/showcase/premature_deaths/poster.pdf
    - icon: pencil
      text: Report
      href: https://www.stat.cmu.edu/cmsac/sure/2024/showcase/premature_deaths/report.html
---

This project examines how socioeconomic factors influence premature death rates across U.S. counties and racial groups.

Using county-level data from the County Health Rankings dataset, I analyzed relationships between premature mortality and indicators such as income inequality, unemployment, and high school completion. I built predictive models in R, comparing multiple linear regression with a gradient boosted tree.

Results show that greater income inequality and unemployment are associated with higher premature mortality, while higher education levels correspond with lower rates. The gradient boosted model achieved the strongest predictive performance and identified high school completion as the most important predictor.
